请将“Things were very different in France. It is true that in 1664, under Louis xiv, a trading company with the exclusive privilege of Transatlantic slave trade had been founded; however, as soon as it was realised that the company was not able to meet overseas demands, the Crown ordered private owners of ships to participate in the slave trade and decreed that they should be exempted from the fee which members of the privileged company had to pay. Additionally, a bonus of 10 livres was offered for every slave imported into the colonies. Following this, new companies were founded. When it had become clear that they could not deliver as many slaves as were demanded by French settlers in the New World, the Crown proclaimed in 1716 that in addition to existing and newly founded companies, all merchants from Le Havre, La Rochelle, Nantes and Bordeaux could engage in legal slave trade upon their own initiative. Those merchants followed suit. Nantes in particular became a city of slave traders. In the course of the eighteenth century, 1,427 voyages were undertaken from the city for the purpose of slave trade (Régent 2007, pp. 40-4). Even after the slave trade had been made illegal in the nineteenth century, merchants from Nantes remained active in the business (Daget 1997, pp. 97-8, quoted in Régent 2007, p. 44).
While French merchants participated in the slave trade even before there were any French colonies in the New World (Régent 2007, p. 57), Dutch merchants only gradually became involved with the trade.42 
However, in contrast to the Portuguese, French, and especially English slave trade, the Dutch slave trade remained relatively limited throughout the period. In the eighteenth century, Dutch ships are reported to have transported 351,000 slaves to the New World, while English ships transported two and a half million (figures in Lovejoy 1996, p. 47).
Nevertheless, everyone who saw the slave trade as an opportunity participated in it. From Alfonso v of Portugal, who in 1472 refused to prohibit the further transport of slaves to Portugal’s overseas’ possessions (Alencastro 1996, p. 99), to the German slave traders organised in the Brandenburgisch-Afrikanischen Kompanie (Lovejoy 1996, p. 48) and the prominent burghers of the ports of New England, one finds ample examples showing that those responsible for the slave trade in the sixteenth, seventeenth, eighteenth and to a large extent nineteenth centuries calculated profits and losses, but there is no evidence that they were worried about the loss of their salvation.
For merchants in the English colonies of North America, getting into the business was legally possible after most privileges had been abolished following the political revolution in England.43 Nevertheless, most ships used for the slave trade continued to be owned by the English. In the eighteenth century alone, more than 2500 ships are reported to have been used for transporting slaves. Nonetheless, the US Congress was well advised to delete the accusation formulated in the draft of the Declaration of Independence Thomas Jefferson had submitted that the English King ‘had waged cruel war against human nature itself, violating its most sacred rights of life and liberty in the persons of a distant people who never offended him, captivating and carrying them into slavery in another hemisphere or to incur miserable death in their transportation hither’ (Jefferson 1787/2002, p. 51). Because going back to the English colonial era in the New World, North American settlers had been very active in the slave trade. As early as 1638, merchants from Massachusetts had become engaged in the slave trade. They were followed shortly by merchants from Rhode Island. Their ships were relatively small and could hardly transport more than twenty slaves. The direct import from Africa continued to take place mostly on big English ships, but owners of ships from New England dominated the regional market and transported two-thirds of the slaves exported from the West Indies to North America.
Following independence, 934 ships from Rhode Island alone were used for the slave trade. For the most part, they acted in the regional slave trade in the West Indies, but a third of their purchases were delivered to North America. According to Steven Deyle, the slave trade became the ‘central aspect’ of New England’s economy (1996, p. 200). And those participating did not have to make any particular efforts to hide this side of their commercial activities from the public. It is true that there were criticisms from Quakers, but otherwise trading in human beings was viewed in New England in the same way as trading with any other good. The social status of merchants and ship owners was a function of their prosperity. Slave traders acted as judges, members in city councils, mayors, governors and later congressmen and senators (Deyle 1996, p. 119). To a certain extent, the social recognition of leading slave traders may have contributed to the slave trade long being regarded as an honourable occupation. If the participation of New England’s slave traders nevertheless continuously fell short of that of English slave traders, as Ronald Bailey has emphasised, this was not due so much to moral considerations as to the economic and political dominance of the competitors. For slave traders of New England, there remained only the less profitable routes and the purchase of slaves in the West Indies, who were then transported to the southern colonies (1992/2007, p. 211).
Just like the English slave traders, many of the slave traders in New England organised their voyages according to the model of triangular trade. Historians referred to a triangular trade in order to explain how the slave trade functioned. The first edge of the triangle was the voyage from Europe to Africa, mostly transporting glass beads and other cheap products to buy slaves. The second edge of the triangle was the so-called ‘middle passage’, the transport of slaves across the Atlantic, particularly to the West Indies. Once the ships arrived, the proceeds from the sale of the slaves were used to buy products, such as molasses, which had been produced by slaves. The final edge of the triangle was the return voyage of the ships with the products from the colonies back to their home ports. After dismissing triangular trade as a myth for a time, historians have come to conclude that not all, but still a vast number of ships went on precisely such voyages.44 This included slave traders from Rhode Island who commenced their voyages in North American ports and invested in spices, rum and molasses in the West Indies before returning to Rhode Island (Coughty 1981, pp. 6-7; quoted in Bailey 1992/2007, p. 210).
Although sometimes European slave traders in Africa themselves engaged in abduction, increasingly local slave traders were regularly used as procurers. This was not only useful for triangular trade, because it made it possible to avoid long layovers in Africa, but also because, if at all possible, the voyage from Africa to the West Indies should take place to the greatest possible extent during the period of trade winds (from December onwards). To replenish their freight on time, the European and American traders thus relied on local suppliers.
From the middle of the seventeenth century, they increasingly not only offered prisoners captured in wars with other African rulers but also captured members of their own tribes (Heywood 2000). David Northrup, who carried out an in-depth study of this trade in southeast Nigeria, concluded that the arrival of European traders did not in fact lead to a fundamental transformation of economic structures, but to an expansion of trade in human beings. This was because to gain possession of goods that could be sold off profitably, the increasingly professional local traders required a substantial supply of slaves. The earlier view that only valueless plunder was ‘paid out’ to African suppliers has by now been abandoned (e.g. by Northrup 1978, p. 226). There were very tangible demands for iron bars, tools, fabrics and particularly weapons. Towards the end of the eighteenth century, English slave traders alone are reported to have introduced three hundred thousand firearms to Africa every year (Blackburn 1997, p. 385).
Not just Europeans and Americans, but also Africans pursued trade with human beings in very much the same way as with any other merchandise of value. According to Northrup, the only difference lay in the violent appropriation of the merchandise at the beginning of the transaction. He wrote that many future slaves were taken prisoner in the course of armed conflict, but others were abducted without any specific reason, and yet others were sold into slavery because of their debt. Once they were sold, they were transported farther to the well-established structures of African slave trade, to be sold and sold on. Selling slaves to foreign traders required substantial organisation. To transport slaves to the landings at which European ships waited, slave traders had to dispose of food, shelter and guards. Though concrete forms of organisation varied from region to region, according to Patrick Manning, the slave trade transformed the ‘economic landscape’ of Africa (1996, p. 74). Although local suppliers must have had an interest in delivering undamaged goods, they were merciless in driving their prisoners to collecting points along the coast.45 
The many who had already died before boarding the ship are not included in the overall figure of 11 to 12 million souls. It is usually estimated today that 1015 percent of prisoners did not survive this stage of their existence as human merchandise.46
And for the eighteenth century, it is estimated that 10-12 percent of prisoners did not survive the voyage. This mortality rate is lower than that of the seamen serving on the slave ships. Of the crews on slave ships that left the port of Nantes between 1644 and 1789, between 17 and 18 percent of seamen did not survive the voyage. This can be explained because after several months the slaves were 
‘unloaded’ while the seamen had to remain on board for the rest of the journey. Between 1670 and 1807 alone, roughly 50,000 English seamen serving on slave ships are estimated to have perished (Etemad 2007, p. 83041 and n42). According to Jean-Marc Masseaut, quite a few seamen serving on French slave ships had to experience prison themselves. When on board, they underwent harsh discipline and discomfort and they had to put up with very poor food. There are no accounts of gestures of solidarity with the human ‘cargo’. Fears of revolts among the captives ruled out compassion. The voyage across the Atlantic was traumatic both for the captives and for the seamen (Masseaut 2002, p. 6ßf.).”翻译为中文

在法国，情况有很大的不同。的确，在1664年，路易十四统治下，成立了一家拥有跨大西洋奴隶贸易专营权的贸易公司；然而，一旦意识到该公司无法满足海外的需求，王室便下令私人船只所有者参与奴隶贸易，并规定他们应免除该特权公司成员所需支付的费用。此外，对每引进殖民地的奴隶，还提供了10里弗的奖励。随后，成立了新的公司。当显然它们也无法提供法国新世界定居者所需的大量奴隶时，王室在1716年宣布，除现有和新成立的公司外，来自勒阿弗尔、拉罗谢尔、南特和波尔多的所有商人都可以自行主动从事合法的奴隶贸易。这些商人随之行动。特别是南特，成为了奴隶贸易商的城市。在十八世纪期间，该城市为奴隶贸易进行了1,427次航行（Régent 2007年，第40-44页）。即使在十九世纪奴隶贸易被宣布为非法后，南特的商人们仍在这一行业中保持活跃（Daget 1997年，第97-98页，引述自Régent 2007年，第44页）。
虽然法国商人在新世界的法国殖民地出现之前就参与了奴隶贸易（Régent 2007年，第57页），但荷兰商人却是逐渐地涉足该贸易。
然而，与葡萄牙、法国，特别是英国的奴隶贸易相比，荷兰的奴隶贸易在整个时期中相对有限。在十八世纪，据报告荷兰船只向新世界运输了351,000名奴隶，而英国船只则运输了两百五十万人（Lovejoy 1996年，第47页的数据）。
尽管如此，凡是将奴隶贸易视为机会的人都参与其中。从1472年拒绝禁止将更多奴隶运往葡萄牙海外领土的葡萄牙的阿方索五世（Alencastro 1996年，第99页），到组织在勃兰登堡-非洲公司中的德国奴隶贸易商（Lovejoy 1996年，第48页），以及新英格兰港口城市的显赫市民，都有充分的例子显示，那些负责十六世纪、十七世纪、十八世纪，以及很大程度上的十九世纪奴隶贸易的人计算着利润和损失，但没有证据表明他们担心会失去他们的救赎。
在北美的英国殖民地的商人，在政治革命后英国取消了大多数特权，法律上有可能进入这一行业。43 然而，用于奴隶贸易的大多数船只仍然是英国人所有。仅在十八世纪，就有超过2500艘船只被报告用于运输奴隶。然而，美国国会非常明智地删除了托马斯·杰斐逊提交的独立宣言草案中所提出的指控，指责英国国王“对人性本身发动了残酷的战争，侵犯了一群从未冒犯过他的远方民众的生命和自由的最神圣权利，将其俘虏并运送到另一个半球的奴役中，或在运输过程中遭受悲惨死亡”（杰斐逊1787/2002年，第51页）。因为回溯到新世界的英国殖民时期，北美的定居者就非常活跃地参与奴隶贸易。早在1638年，马萨诸塞州的商人就参与了奴隶贸易。罗德岛的商人紧随其后。他们的船只相对较小，几乎无法运输超过二十名奴隶。从非洲的直接进口主要还是通过大型的英国船只进行，但来自新英格兰的船只拥有者在区域市场上占据主导地位，运输了从西印度群岛出口到北美的三分之二的奴隶。
独立后，仅罗德岛就有934艘船只用于奴隶贸易。他们主要活动在西印度群岛的区域奴隶贸易中，但他们购买的三分之一被运送到北美。据Steven Deyle称，奴隶贸易成为了新英格兰经济的“核心方面”（1996年，第200页）。而那些参与者无需在公众面前特别掩饰这一商业活动的一面。的确，虽然收到了贵格会者的批评，但在新英格兰，与其他任何商品的交易一样，进行人口贸易被视为正常。商人和船主的社会地位是他们繁荣程度的体现。奴隶贸易商担任法官、市议会成员、市长、州长，后来还有国会议员和参议员的职务（Deyle 1996年，第119页）。在一定程度上，领先的奴隶贸易商的社会认可可能促进了奴隶贸易长期被视为一种光荣的职业。如果新英格兰的奴隶贸易商的参与度仍然低于英国奴隶贸易商，正如罗纳德·贝利所强调的，这并不是出于道德考虑，而是因为竞争对手的经济和政治支配地位。对于新英格兰的奴隶贸易商来说，他们只剩下了利润较低的路线和在西印度群岛购买奴隶，然后将他们运往南部殖民地（1992/2007年，第211页）。
就像英国奴隶贸易商一样，许多新英格兰的奴隶贸易商也按照三角贸易模式组织他们的航行。历史学家提到三角贸易是为了解释奴隶贸易是如何运作的。三角形的第一边是从欧洲到非洲的航行，主要运输玻璃珠和其他廉价产品以购买奴隶。三角形的第二边是所谓的“中间航程”，即将奴隶跨大西洋运输，特别是到西印度群岛。一旦船只到达，就使用奴隶销售所得来购买诸如糖蜜等由奴隶生产的产品。三角形的最后一边是船只载着殖民地产品返回它们的母港。在一段时间内将三角贸易视为一个神话之后，历史学家们已经得出结论，不是所有，但仍然有大量的船只进行了确切这样的航行。44这包括从罗德岛出发的奴隶贸易商，他们从北美港口开始航行，在西印度群岛投资于香料、朗姆酒和糖蜜，然后返回罗德岛（Coughty 1981年，第6-7页；引述自Bailey 1992/2007年，第210页）。
尽管有时欧洲奴隶贸易商在非洲自己参与了绑架，但越来越多地，定期使用当地奴隶贸易商作为采购者。这不仅对三角贸易有用，因为它使得避免在非洲长时间停留成为可能，而且还因为，如果可能的话，从非洲到西印度群岛的航行应该尽可能在贸易风季节（从12月开始）进行。为了及时补充他们的货物，欧洲和美国的贸易商因此依赖本地供应商。
从十七世纪中叶开始，他们越来越不仅提供与其他非洲统治者交战中捕获的囚犯，还包括捕获的本族成员（Heywood 2000年）。David Northrup对这一贸易在东南尼日利亚进行了深入研究，他得出结论称，欧洲贸易商的到来实际上并没有导致经济结构的根本变革，而是导致了人口贸易的扩展。这是因为为了获得可以盈利销售的商品，日益专业化的本地贸易商需要大量的奴隶供应。早期观点认为，只有没有价值的战利品被“支付”给非洲供应商，现在已经被放弃了（例如Northrup 1978年，第226页）。非洲贸易商对铁条、工具、织物，尤其是武器有着非常具体的需求。到了十八世纪末，据报告英国奴隶贸易商每年单是向非洲引进三十万支火枪（Blackburn 1997年，第385页）。
不仅是欧洲人和美国人，非洲人也以非常相同的方式从事人口贸易，就像与任何其他有价值的商品一样。据Northrup称，唯一的区别在于交易开始时对商品的暴力占有。他写道，许多未来的奴隶是在武装冲突中被俘的，但其他人是在没有任何特定原因的情况下被绑架的，还有些人是因为债务而被卖为奴隶。一旦他们被卖出，就被运往到非洲奴隶贸易的成熟结构中，继续被销售。将奴隶卖给外国贸易商需要大量的组织。为了将奴隶运输到欧洲船只等待的登陆点，奴隶贸易商必须准备食物、住所和守卫。Patrick Manning表示，尽管具体的组织形式因地区而异，奴隶贸易改变了非洲的“经济景观”（1996年，第74页）。尽管当地供应商必须对交付无损商品有兴趣，但他们在将囚犯驱赶到沿海的集结点时毫不留情。
在登船前已经死亡的许多人并未包含在1100万到1200万灵魂的总数中。今天通常估计，15到15％的囚犯无法在作为人类商品的这一阶段生存下来。
至于十八世纪，估计有10-12％的囚犯未能在航行中生存下来。这一死亡率低于奴隶船上的海员。在1644年到1789年间离开南特港的奴隶船上，17到18％的海员未能生还。这可以解释为，因为在几个月后奴隶被“卸载”，而海员们必须在剩余的旅程中留在船上。仅在1670年到1807年间，据估计大约有50,000名在奴隶船上服务的英国海员丧生（Etemad 2007年，第83041和备注42）。据Jean-Marc Masseaut称，不少在法国奴隶船上服务的海员自己也必须经历监禁。在船上，他们经历了严格的纪律和不适，并且不得不忍受非常恶劣的食物。没有人谈及与人“货物”之间的同情行为。对囚犯叛乱的恐惧排除了同情心。横渡大西洋的航行对于囚犯和海员来说都是一次创伤性体验（Masseaut 2002年，第6ßf）。

请将“After the prohibition of the slave trade, even more captives were penned up together on ships. According to contemporary reports, often as many as 500 or even 900 prisoners were now packed onto a ship (Behanzin 2002, p. 113). And slave traders also decided to buy youths because they took up less space than adults. In his memoires published in 1854, Théodore Canot described the method he used to load the ‘freight’ on board. The slaves ‘on the right-hand side of the ship were turned forward so that they had their backs turned to their neighbour, while those on the left-hand side of the ship were stored with their right-hand side facing the stern ... This way, all of them laid down on the righthand side of their body’. It was assumed that this was salutary for the heart (Canot, quoted in Behanzin 2002, p. 112, transl.). Even if we may suppose that not all illegally acting slave traders used such a system, the share of captives who did not survive the journey rose. In the nineteenth century, it was reckoned to be as high as 25 percent (Behanzin 2002, p. 113). Such statistics are always uncertain; suffice it to say that it is possible that even more prisoners than in earlier times did not survive the transport.
In the period immediately following the unloading of the human freight, many other deaths occurred, because children, women and men did not survive the practices used to ‘adapt’ them to the condition of slavery. The term used in English is ‘seasoning’, and it is probably no coincidence that this term also applies to smoking in a pipe.
Even if the usual explanation of triangular trade was more of an economic model than the result of thorough empirical research, it is easy to understand that the profitable trade in slaves presupposed a sort of trade not just for the leg from Africa to the colonies, but also for the return journey. Because Dutch slave traders in the second half of the eighteenth century were often forced to merely add ballast for their return trip, they made 5-10 percent profit at best and often even made losses (Blackburn 1997, p. 389). If profits in the slave trade did not necessarily depend upon triangular trade, they did depend on having freight for each leg of the journey. More specifically, they depended on the prices that African suppliers required for their slaves and the prices that slaves could fetch 
overseas once they had been unloaded. For the latter, expectations regarding the sale of products from the colonies in the mother country were decisive. We shall return to the question of the importance of profits in colonial trade for the development of industrial capitalism in the European mother countries. Suffice it for the time being to point out that Transatlantic trade in human beings created the prerequisites for exploiting slave labour in the colonies and thus potential profits for slave owners and slave traders.
As for the continued demand of imports of slaves after the prohibition of the slave trade, it depended upon three factors: the extension of production for which slaves were used, demand for products from the colonies in Europe and in other colonies and the biological reproduction of slaves who had already been imported.47
In 1807, the English government prohibited the slave traded8 A year later, the United States of America followed its example.49 At the behest of the English government, a general prohibition was decided on at the Vienna Congress in 
1815. Over the following years, the British navy was used to ensure compliance with this prohibition. This, however, required that the country under whose flag a ship was sailing had declared that it agreed to such control measures. In 1841, this understanding between England, France, Prussia, Austria and Russia was concluded in the so-called Quintuple Treaty of 1839. As early as 1835 a treaty between Spain and England had made the transport of slaves under the Spanish flag illegal. Spanish ship owners who did not wish to stop trading in slaves thus used a ‘flag of convenience’, i. e. in this case the flag of a country like the USA and Portugal which had not declared themselves in agreement with English controls (Eltis and Richardson 2008, p. 37). There can be no doubt that the military and diplomatic engagement of England had been motivated by the repeated calls for prohibition by abolitionists, but there can equally be no doubt that it was also used to reinforce England’s position of power over the high seas, particularly the Atlantic. The fight against the slave trade allowed England to establish its authority as maritime police force (Bülck 1953, p. 20f.).
Despite such controls, from 1821 to 1830 more slaves were shipped out of Africa than at any period prior to 1780 (Eltis 2001/2009, p. 199; Eltis and Richardson 2008, Table 1.6, pp. 40-1). This was first of all due to the supply. Because despite the emergence of a new export item - palm oil - slave trade continued to be practised in Africa. This particularly applies to southeast Nigeria, one of the most important regions of origin for the Atlantic slave trade. The drop in the slave trade that had started in the 1780s was only sustained on the Gold Coast and in Senegal (Eltis 2001/2009, pp. 200f.).
Prohibitions and control measures did not lead to the disappearance of the Transatlantic slave trade, but did turn it into a riskier business, thus threatening profit margins despite the high prices to be obtained for slaves in the West Indies and the United States.50
While it is true that European powers fought the Transatlantic slave trade, the leading officials of the colonial powers chose to ignore the slave trade within Africa and the exporting of slaves to the East. And that is not all. Because all colonial powers introduced forms of forced labour and allowed necessary labourers to be supplied by chiefs, they reinforced the continued existence of slavery in Africa. It was not until the twentieth century, and then only in West Africa, that the African slave trade declined in significance (Coquery- Vidrovitch 2002, p. 25).
First Theoretical Remark: On Merchants and Capitalists
For Fernand Braudel, Henri Pirenne, Immanuel Wallerstein, Walter Sweezy and many others, those merchants who launched the development of world trade from Europe since the fifteenth century were the midwives of capitalism.51 Braudel thought that yesterday’s capitalism emerged when individual merchants went beyond the rules of trading practices, which had been developed for many goods in Europe in early modernity (1979/1986; Trade, p. 50I).52 In other words, in Braudel’s view, the merchants’ desire for profit, a psychological predisposition, was the beginning of capitalism.
Reports on individual trading companies suggest that such a predisposition existed. But that in itself does not transform actors in world trade into capitalists, since their opportunities for making profits were based upon privileges granted by princes. Trading companies petitioned for the privilege of engaging in trade in a certain region of the world, free from competition. They paid for that privilege in advance and/or with the confirmation that the prince would get a share of the expected profité3 But because their privileges could only 
be maintained using armed violence to oppose privileges granted by other princes, competition between European princes was transferred to the seas. European long-distance traders were merchant warriors.
Privileges were the institutional expression of a convergence of the interests of merchants and princes. They anchored privileged merchants engaged in long-distance trade firmly in the structures of the Ancien Régime. Since monopolies in trading in certain areas of the world could not be granted twice or thrice by European rulers, the number of privileges to be awarded by princes was limited. In light of the meagre prospects of acquiring such privileges themselves, new players on the market called for the complete abolition of the system. In 1649, it was abolished in the United Kingdom and in 1789 it was abolished in France.
The Ancien Régime of European long-distance trade broke under the weight of its own contradictions. Because the cost of armed competition with other trading companies and the costs of violence in all overseas regions reduced profit margins considerably, in the course of time almost all trading companies which were specialised in appropriation overseas went bankrupt.
Of course, that did not prevent individual merchants or employees of overseas trading companies from accumulating vast amounts of wealth. To the extent they did not consume their wealth,54 they invested it for the most part in social climbing, especially in land ownership. As early as 1971, Paul Bairoch 
observed that merchants (whom he referred to as l’ancienne classe capitaliste) only had a minimal influence upon the development of industrialisation (1971/1992, p. 69). Thereafter, this view was also defended by other authors (e.g. Kindleberger 1975, pp. 613-54). 
CHAPTER 3
Historical Preconditions for Capitalist Accumulation
Competition Set Free
Capitalism requires means of production, i.e. ‘capital’, as well as labourers who carry out the instructions of the owners of the means of production or their rep-resentatives. But wage relations had existed previously, and capitalism did not come about simply when the number of wage workers attained an unprecedented level. For capitalism is an economic system the particular dynamics of which are based upon competition. Competition between producers induces them to reduce costs and/or improve their production, whether through technology or organisation or by stepping up the exploitation of living labour, at the risk of losing their economic position.
Competition is the basis of the functioning of capitalist markets. While markets existed long before and outside capitalist structures, in many respects competition on markets was limited through their regulation by local and regional authorities, both through the granting of trading privileges and by sanctioning limits to production. Many regulations were only sanctioned by authorities while their definition had been left to corporations that had been privileged by the authorities - trading companies and guilds in particular. The permission to carry out activities in certain branches of trade and crafts and the authorised extent of activities in the crafts were regulated. As for the labourers, not just their wages were regulated, but they were subjected to the rule of authorities, immediate domination by their employers and (police) control by local authorities.
When capitalism came to reign, such regulation was fought and ultimately abolished. Karl Polanyi suggested terming this setting free of labour relations from political rule as ‘disembedding’ (1944/1995). I use the term ‘freeing competition’ and I do not wholly agree with Polanyi’s argument. Because while it is true that commercial liberties were one of the preconditions for the development of capitalism, this did not necessarily imply the freeing of labour relations from legal regulation. ‘Free wage labour’, and this is one of the central propositions of this study, was not and is not a prerequisite for capitalist production. The fact that it now prevails throughout the world has been brought about by political struggle. In the second part of this chapter, I shall explain how ‘free 
wage labour’ emerged in England, France and Germany. In the third part of this chapter, I shall present why the development of labour relations in the United States lies in contradiction with equating capitalism and free wage labour.”翻译为中文

在奴隶贸易被禁止后，更多的俘虏被关在船上。根据当时的报道，经常有多达500甚至900名囚犯被挤在一艘船上（Behanzin 2002年，第113页）。奴隶贩子还决定购买青少年，因为他们占用的空间比成人少。Théodore Canot在1854年出版的回忆录中描述了他用来装载“货物”的方法。船上靠右侧的奴隶被转向前方，背对着他们的邻居，而靠左侧的奴隶则将右侧面向船尾……这样，他们都躺在身体的右侧。人们认为这对心脏有益（Canot, 引述自 Behanzin 2002年，第112页，译者注）。即使我们可能认为，并非所有非法行动的奴隶贩子都使用了这样的系统，但无法生还旅程的俘虏比例升高了。在十九世纪，这一比例被认为高达25%（Behanzin 2002年，第113页）。这样的统计数据总是不确定的；可以说，相比以往，可能有更多的囚犯无法生还运输。

在卸载人类货物后的一段时间内，还发生了许多其他死亡，因为儿童、妇女和男人无法生存下来，以适应奴役条件所使用的做法。“适应”这个词在英语中使用的术语是“调味”，这或许不是偶然的，因为这个术语也适用于烟斗中的吸烟过程。

即使通常对三角贸易的解释更多是一个经济模型而不是彻底的经验研究的结果，但很容易理解，有利可图的奴隶贸易不仅假设了从非洲到殖民地的一段贸易，而且也假设了返回旅程的贸易。因为十八世纪下半叶的荷兰奴隶贩子在返航旅程中常常被迫仅增加压舱物，他们最多只能获得5-10%的利润，而且经常还会亏损（Blackburn 1997年，第389页）。如果奴隶贸易的利润不一定依赖于三角贸易，但它们确实依赖于每一段旅程的货物。更具体地说，它们依赖于非洲供应商要求的奴隶价格以及奴隶在卸货后海外能够卖出的价格。对于后者，决定性的因素是母国关于殖民地产品销售的预期。

至于在奴隶贸易被禁止后奴隶进口需求的持续，它取决于三个因素：使用奴隶的生产扩展、欧洲和其他殖民地对殖民地产品的需求，以及已经进口的奴隶的生物繁殖。47

1807年，英国政府禁止了奴隶贸易。48一年后，美利坚合众国效仿其例。49在英国政府的要求下，1815年维也纳会议上决定了全面禁止。在接下来的几年中，英国海军被用来确保遵守这一禁令。然而，这要求船只所属国家宣布同意这种控制措施。在1841年，这种英国、法国、普鲁士、奥地利和俄罗斯之间的理解在所谓的1839年五国条约中得到了落实。早在1835年，西班牙和英国之间的条约就已经使得在西班牙旗下运输奴隶非法化。因此，那些不希望停止奴隶贸易的西班牙船东使用了“方便旗”，即在这种情况下，使用了像美国和葡萄牙这样尚未同意英国控制的国家的旗帜（Eltis和Richardson 2008年，第37页）。毫无疑问，英国的军事和外交参与是由废奴主义者的反复呼吁所激发的，但同样毫无疑问，它也被用来加强英国在公海上，尤其是在大西洋上的权力地位。打击奴隶贸易使得英国得以建立其海上警察的权威（Bülck 1953年，第20f页）。

尽管有这样的控制，从1821年到1830年，自1780年以前的任何时期都有更多的奴隶从非洲运出（Eltis 2001/2009年，第199页；Eltis和Richardson 2008年，表1.6，第40-41页）。这首先是由于供应的原因。因为尽管出现了一种新的出口商品——棕榈油——但在非洲，奴隶贸易继续被实践。这特别适用于东南尼日利亚，这是大西洋奴隶贸易最重要的起源地之一。自1780年代开始的奴隶贸易减少只在黄金海岸和塞内加尔得以持续（Eltis 2001/2009年，第200f页）。

禁令和控制措施并未导致跨大西洋奴隶贸易的消失，但确实将其变成了一项风险更高的生意，尽管在西印度群岛和美国能够为奴隶获得高价，但仍威胁着利润率。50

虽然欧洲列强确实打击了跨大西洋奴隶贸易，但殖民地官员选择忽视非洲内部的奴隶贸易以及向东方出口奴隶的行为。而且不仅如此。因为所有殖民列强引入了强迫劳动形式，并允许通过酋长提供所需的劳工，他们加强了奴隶制在非洲的持续存在。直到二十世纪，然后只是在西非，非洲的奴隶贸易才在重要性上有所下降（Coquery-Vidrovitch 2002年，第25页）。

第一次理论性评论：关于商人和资本家

对于费尔南德·布罗代尔、亨利·皮伦、伊曼纽尔·沃勒斯坦、沃尔特·斯威齐以及许多其他人来说，自十五世纪以来从欧洲开启世界贸易发展的商人是资本主义的助产士。51布罗代尔认为，当个体商人超越了早期现代欧洲为许多商品发展的贸易实践规则时，昨日的资本主义就出现了（1979/1986；贸易，第50I页）。52换句话说，在布罗代尔看来，商人对利润的渴望，一种心理倾向，是资本主义的开始。

关于个体贸易公司的报告表明，这种倾向是存在的。但这本身并不会将世界贸易中的行为者转变为资本家，因为他们获利的机会是基于王侯授予的特权。贸易公司为了获取在世界某个地区从事贸易的特权而请愿，免于竞争。他们提前支付这种特权费用和/或确认王侯将获得预期利润的一份额。但因为他们的特权只能通过使用武力对抗其他王侯授予的特权来维持，欧洲王侯之间的竞争被转移到了海上。欧洲远程贸易商是商人战士。

特权是商人和王侯利益一致性的制度表达。它们将从事远距离贸易的特权商人牢牢地锚定在旧制度的结构中。由于欧洲统治者不能二次或三次授予某个世界区域的贸易垄断权，因此王侯可授予的特权数量是有限的。鉴于自己获得这种特权的前景微乎其微，市场上的新玩家呼吁完全废除这一制度。1649年，它在英国被废除，在1789年在法国被废除。

欧洲远程贸易的旧制度因其内在矛盾的重压而崩溃。因为与其他贸易公司的武装竞争以及在所有海外地区的暴力行为的成本大幅降低了利润率，随着时间的推移，几乎所有专门从事海外掠夺的贸易公司都破产了。

当然，这并不妨碍个别商人或海外贸易公司的雇员积累大量财富。在他们没有消费掉财富的情况下，他们大部分投资于社会地位的提升，特别是土地所有权。早在1971年，保罗·巴罗就观察到，商人（他称之为l’ancienne classe capitaliste，即“旧资本家阶级”）对工业化的发展只有最小的影响（1971/1992年，第69页）。此后，其他作者也持有这一观点（例如Kindleberger 1975年，第613-54页）。

第三章
资本主义积累的历史前提条件
释放竞争

资本主义需要生产资料，即“资本”，以及执行生产资料所有者或其代表指令的劳工。但工资关系以前就存在了，并且当工资工人的数量达到前所未有的水平时，资本主义并没有就此产生。因为资本主义是一个其特定动力基于竞争的经济体系。生产者之间的竞争促使他们降低成本和/或改进生产，无论是通过技术还是组织，或者通过加强对活劳动的剥削，以免失去他们的经济地位。

竞争是资本主义市场运作的基础。虽然市场在资本主义结构之前和之外就长期存在，但在很多方面，市场上的竞争通过当地和地区当局的规制而受到限制，包括通过授予贸易特权和制裁生产限制。许多规章只是由当局制裁，而其定义则留给了由当局赋予特权的公司——特别是贸易公司和行会。在某些贸易和手工艺领域进行活动的许可，以及手工艺活动的授权范围均受到了管制。对于劳工而言，不仅他们的工资受到了管制，而且他们还受到当局的统治，雇主的直接支配以及（警察）当地当局的控制。

当资本主义开始统治时，这种规制被抗争并最终被废除。卡尔·波兰尼建议将劳动关系从政治统治中脱钩的过程称为“脱嵌”（1944/1995）。我使用“释放竞争”一词，我并不完全同意波兰尼的论点。因为虽然商业自由是资本主义发展的前提条件之一，但这并不一定意味着劳动关系要从法律调节中解放出来。“自由工资劳动”，正如本研究的一个中心命题所述，并不是也从未是资本主义生产的前提条件。现在它在全世界普遍盛行，是通过政治斗争实现的。在本章的第二部分中，我将解释“自由工资劳动”是如何在英国、法国和德国出现的。在本章的第三部分中，我将展示为什么在美国劳动关系的发展与将资本主义和自由工资劳动等同起来形成了矛盾。

请将“Commercial Freedom Ashore
Just like producers who were unable to gain access to a guild or to acquire the permit to run a factory, merchants who were not members of a privileged trading company demanded commercial freedom. They were granted such freedoms in the course of those revolutionary political processes rightfully referred to as ‘bourgeois revolutions’ inasmuch as developments corresponded to the claims of wealthy and less wealthy members of the bourgeois citizenry.1
With the disappearance of politically awarded privileges (monopolies) (almost) all actors engaged in trade were subject to competition. Commercial freedom - or the disembedding of the market from domination and thus by definition from relations of violence - thus created legal conditions for capitalism to develop. Irrespective of the extent to which these newly created opportunities were actually used, commercial freedom led to a change in the meaning of the term ‘monopoly’. It was no longer a legal position awarded by a prince but the result of market processes, to which everyone was subject who fulfilled the legal conditions for exercising a trade. There were still preconditions for accessing some trades, but with the abolition of guilds and privileged trading companies, it was no longer corporations but individuals who had legal access to the market. They were thus subject to competition from other market participants and therefore lived under conditions which Max Weber has called ‘slavery without masters’ (transl.) (1922/1976, p. 709). Competition leads competitors to prevent their market chances from worsening by practicing and developing strategies and consequently measures for improving their market chances. One of these measures has been and still is the aggravation of the exploitation of labour, either by intensifying labour or by reducing wages.
Once capitalism has been set in motion, it not only continuously integrates new areas into market processes, but also leads to an expansion in volumes of production. This makes it necessary to expand existing and to open up new markets. And this requires transport, not only cheap transport,2 but also trans- 
port which is as safe as possible. The development of capitalist production was thus accompanied by the endeavour of pacifying transport routes. In this very specific respect, the development of capitalism was indeed accompanied by a decrease of direct violence in the context of practices of appropriation.
Commercial Freedom on the High Seas
Up until well into the seventeenth century, the practice of armed violence char-acterised the transport of goods on ships. This was caused, on the one hand, by European long-distance traders who carried out raids for booty against their competitors; on the other, by European princes who sought to exclude the subjects of other rulers from trade with their own overseas possessions. For this purpose, they claimed domination over certain regions of the world’s seas. This claim was summed up with the expression ‘mare clausum’. Even if it was only possible to achieve this domination to a very limited extent, whosoever violated territorial rights had to expect to be held to answer as a smuggler and to possibly lose not just their entire cargo, but potentially their ship as well. This notwithstanding, smuggling continued to exist.
Since the seventeenth century, mare clausum was not just undermined by smuggling but also by bilateral trading treaties. And since the independence of the English colonies in North America, it was no longer possible to impose the principle. Even if the freedom of the seas had not been formally decided on prior to the UN’s adopting the ‘Law of the Ocean’ in 1982, it had existed de facto since the end of the eighteenth century.3
Smuggling acted as a complement to official trading routes in times of war and in times of peace and to a certain extent ensured competition between goods that were supplied both lawfully and illicitly but smuggling had not yet created competition within maritime transport. Such competition could only develop when ship owners were able to compete with one another by making use of cheap, and later also of fast transport.
However, the factual end of mare clausum meant, for instance, that a ship sailing under the flag of Prussia could go to London, legally pick up freight and legally transport it to North or South America. And because seamen on Prussian ships were particularly badly paid, Prussian shipowners were increasingly able to successfully snatch freight from long established competitors.
But commercial freedom at sea only applied during times of peace. Since the belligerent parties denied one another this freedom during wars, they thereby 
created opportunities for the trade and for the sea transport of neutral states. This opportunity was quite often also put to advantage by owners of ships in the belligerent countries by flying a neutral flag on their ships throughout the war. It is true that the authorities required skippers to become citizens, but as early as the seventeenth and throughout the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries settlements were occasionally faked.
Nevertheless, a neutral flag was not always entirely reliable. It is true that from the end of the eighteenth century, the principle of ‘free ship, free good’ came to prevail, meaning that a neutral flag served as protection for all nonmilitary goods being carried irrespective of whether the owners were subjects of a neutral or enemy power. However, England insisted that private property to be found on neutral ships, if the property of enemies, could be taken as a prize, and it went so far that if an enemy cargo was on board the entire ship could be taken as a prize. This interpretation of law, known to legal historians as consolât el mar, meant that ships under a neutral flag were regularly subject to searches and also meant that when war broke out, insurance contributions for neutral ships skyrocketed. At any rate, consolât el mar was of little help to shipowners in weak states because they had to reckon with being occupied by one of the belligerent powers at least temporarily.4 
Since roughly 1830, the practice of European maritime shipping changed. While the port of destination of ships had previously been determined at their home port, returning home directly or indirectly by way of another port of call, international maritime transport increasingly adapted to the requirements of international business. Ships with freight or ballast were often sent off on tramp shipping expeditions which lasted many years.5 At the same time, international competition developed in sea transport. Part of the competition was based on price, but increasingly, even in the age of sailing ships, also on speed.6 It was only once the principle of mare librum was established and ensuing international competition had become possible that seafaring became part and parcel of the capitalist economy.
The Pacification of Transport Routes
With the expansion of the production of commodities and of international trade, the need arose to protect private property against robbery during its transportation.
The Fight against Highway Robbery
Up until well into the nineteenth century, there were robbers in many regions later under the sway of capitalism who acted as ‘impediments’ to orderly commerce. It is true that in some areas such as the south of France and England large bands of robbers were seldom seen, but in Central Europe of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries they were relatively frequent. Most of these robbers, particularly the notorious ‘Big Dutch Band’, focused on burglary, using crowbars or even battering rams to gain access to buildings they had scouted out. But here, we shall focus on highway robbery. It was a specialty of the ‘Großen Siechenbande’ or ‘Great Sickly Band’, because their members came almost without exception from leper houses around Düsseldorf (Finzsch 1990, p. 244). But bands out for loot in the Netherlands and in French and Prussian possessions at the end of the eighteenth and the beginning of the nineteenth 
century also occasionally attacked voyagers. According to reports, their booty was often small and the fences on whom such robbers depended required their share of the loot.7
Here, we are only concerned with the potential threat for transport and thus for trading companies. I shall therefore ignore the social origins of the robbers8 or the idealisation of noble ‘Robin Hoods’9 who were widespread in the age of robbery.10 In southern Italy recourse of big families to relatives to help them use robbery to make ends meet did not end until the beginning of the twentieth century, whereas in the Rhineland it had ended in the early nineteenth century. For most historians, the breaking up of the Schinderhannes band in 1803 was the end of big banditry in the Rhineland. It is true that there were still attacks by smaller groups of robbers (Finzsch 1990, p. 279), but as the police forces were developed and military and special courts were used and the French and Prussian authorities began to cooperate, roads in the Rhineland became relatively safe.11 
In contrast to the regions near the Rhine which were administered by the French, the reinforcement of the state power in both other regions of France as well as in the United States was only very gradual in the nineteenth century. In some regions of the United States, voyagers were less likely to be robbed by bands because the bands had specialised in more lucrative operations such as bank robberies or robbing cash transports.
It is true that robberies were sometimes understood as robbery of the rich and secretly excused, but according to Eric Hobsbawm the idealising of bandits, save some exceptions, ended when capitalism came to prevail in agriculture (2007/2000, pp. 198-200).
Fighting Piracy
Robbery was more commonplace on the seas than on land - not least because European governments often had their share in piracy.12 This changed when war was declared on North African pirates. With the blessing of and for the benefit of their rulers, seamen from North Africa on the Mediterranean hunted down merchant ships. Their aim was less the cargo than the crew, who were imprisoned and enslaved. They were released on ransom (Bono 2004/2009, p. 249). Even if their raids aimed at imprisoning seamen rather than stealing cargo, these pirates also did damage to the maritime transport of goods.
If European diplomats were prepared to pay ransom for imprisoned seamen, they were obliged to negotiate with the regents in whose interests the piracy was carried out. They had to agree to pay. Over the years peace treaties and pacts of non-aggression were concluded between North African rulers and individual European consuls?3 That was tantamount to the de facto recognition of the North African regents (deys) as lords of quasi-state structures which came to be known in Europe as the ‘Barbary’ states from the seventeenth century onwards. Officially, their regents were acting on behalf of the Ottoman Empire, but in fact they were for the most part acting independently.”翻译为中文

陆地上的商业自由

就像无法加入行会或获得开办工厂许可的生产者一样，非特权贸易公司成员的商人也要求商业自由。这些自由在被正当称为“资产阶级革命”的革命政治过程中被授予，因为发展符合富裕和不那么富裕的资产阶级公民成员的要求。随着政治授予的特权（垄断）的消失（几乎）所有从事贸易的行为者都受到了竞争的约束。商业自由——或者说市场从支配中解放出来，因此从定义上脱离了暴力关系——因此为资本主义的发展创造了法律条件。不管这些新创造的机会被实际利用的程度如何，商业自由导致了“垄断”一词含义的变化。它不再是王侯授予的法律地位，而是市场过程的结果，所有符合行使贸易法律条件的人都受其约束。进入某些行业仍有先决条件，但随着行会和特权贸易公司的废除，进入市场的法律通道不再是公司而是个人。因此，他们受到其他市场参与者的竞争，因此生活在马克斯·韦伯所说的“无主的奴役”条件下（转述）（1922/1976年，第709页）。竞争促使竞争者通过实践和发展战略来防止他们的市场机会恶化，因此采取提高市场机会的措施。这些措施之一过去和现在仍然是加剧对劳动力的剥削，要么通过加强劳动力，要么通过降低工资。

一旦资本主义启动，它不仅持续将新领域纳入市场过程，而且还导致生产量的扩大。这就需要扩大现有市场和开拓新市场。这就需要运输，不仅是便宜的运输，还需要尽可能安全的运输。因此，资本主义生产的发展伴随着使运输路线和平化的努力。在这个非常具体的方面，资本主义的发展确实伴随着对占有实践中直接暴力的减少。

高海上的商业自由

直到17世纪深入，武装暴力的实践还主宰着船只上的货物运输。一方面，这是由欧洲远距离贸易商对其竞争对手进行掠夺性袭击造成的；另一方面，由于欧洲君主试图排除其他统治者的臣民与其海外领地的贸易。为此，他们宣称对世界海域某些区域的统治。这一主张用“封闭海域”一词概括。即使这种统治只能在非常有限的程度上实现，任何侵犯领土权利的行为都必须预料到会被当作走私者处理，可能不仅失去全部货物，甚至可能失去船只。尽管如此，走私活动仍然存在。

自17世纪以来，除了走私活动外，“封闭海域”还受到双边贸易条约的削弱。自北美英国殖民地独立以来，就不再可能强加这一原则。即使在1982年联合国通过“海洋法”之前，海洋自由并未正式决定，但自18世纪末以来事实上已经存在。

走私是在战争和和平时期对官方贸易路线的补充，在一定程度上确保了合法供应和非法供应的商品之间的竞争，但走私还未在海上运输中创造竞争。只有当船东能够通过利用便宜，后来还包括快速运输来相互竞争时，这种竞争才有可能发展。

然而，“封闭海域”的实际结束意味着，例如，在普鲁士旗帜下航行的船只可以合法地前往伦敦，合法地装载货物并将其运往北美或南美。由于普鲁士船上的海员工资特别低，普鲁士船东越来越能够成功地从长期建立的竞争对手手中抢夺货运。

但是，海上的商业自由只适用于和平时期。由于交战方在战争期间相互否认这一自由，因此为中立国的贸易和海上运输创造了机会。这一机会很多时候也被交战国的船东利用，他们通过在全战期间为他们的船只悬挂中立国旗帜。的确，当局要求船长成为公民，但早在17世纪和整个18世纪及19世纪，有时居住地是伪造的。

尽管如此，中立国旗帜并不总是完全可靠。的确，从18世纪末开始，"自由船，自由货物"原则开始盛行，意味着中立国旗帜为搭载的所有非军事货物提供了保护，无论货物所有者是中立国还是敌对国的臣民。然而，英国坚持认为，可在中立船只上发现的私人财产，如果是敌人的财产，可以被视为战利品，甚至走得更远，如果船上有敌人的货物，整艘船都可以被视为战利品。这种法律解释，在法律史家中被称为“consolât el mar”，意味着中立国旗船只经常受到搜查，这也意味着一旦战争爆发，中立船只的保险费用会飙升。无论如何，“consolât el mar”对弱小国家的船东帮助不大，因为他们必须预计至少会暂时被其中一个交战国占领。

自大约1830年起，欧洲海运的实践发生了变化。虽然之前船只的目的港是在其母港决定的，直接或间接通过另一个港口返回家园，国际海运逐渐适应了国际商业的要求。装有货物或压舱物的船只经常被派遣进行为期多年的随机航运。同时，海上运输中出现了国际竞争。竞争的一部分基于价格，但即使在帆船时代，也越来越多地基于速度。只有在“自由海域”原则确立并随后出现国际竞争之后，海运才成为资本主义经济的一部分。

运输路线的和平化

随着商品生产和国际贸易的扩展，出现了在运输过程中保护私人财产免受抢劫的需求。

与公路抢劫的斗争

直至19世纪深入，许多后来受资本主义支配的地区都有抢劫者，他们作为对有序商业的“阻碍”行动。的确，像法国南部和英国这样的一些地区很少见到大型抢劫团伙，但在17世纪和18世纪的中欧，他们相对频繁。这些抢劫者中的大多数，特别是臭名昭著的“大荷兰帮”，专注于入室盗窃，使用撬棒甚至撞锤闯入他们侦查过的建筑物。但在这里，我们将关注公路抢劫。这是“Großen Siechenbande”或“大瘟疫帮”的特长，因为他们的成员几乎都来自杜塞尔多夫周围的麻风病院（Finzsch 1990年，第244页）。但在18世纪末和19世纪初，荷兰和法国以及普鲁売领地的掠夺团伙偶尔也会袭击旅行者。据报道，他们的赃物通常很少，依赖这些抢劫者的黑市交易商需要分一杯羹。

在这里，我们只关心对运输和因此对贸易公司构成的潜在威胁。因此，我将忽略抢劫者的社会起源或被普遍理想化的贵族“罗宾汉”，他们在抢劫时代广受欢迎。在意大利南部，大家族依靠亲戚帮助他们通过抢劫维生的做法一直持续到20世纪初，而在莱茵兰则在19世纪早期结束。对大多数历史学家来说，1803年“Schinderhannes”团伙的瓦解标志着莱茵兰大型匪帮的终结。的确，仍然有小型抢劫团伙的袭击（Finzsch 1990年，第279页），但随着警察力量的发展，军事和特别法庭的使用，以及法国和普鲁士当局开始合作，莱茵兰的道路变得相对安全。

与莱茵附近由法国管理的地区相比，19世纪整个法国以及美国其他地区的国家权力加强只是非常缓慢的过程。在美国的一些地区，旅行者不太可能被团伙抢劫，因为这些团伙专门从事更有利可图的行动，如银行抢劫或抢劫现金运输。

的确，抢劫有时被理解为对富人的抢劫并被秘密谅解，但据埃里克·霍布斯鲍姆说，除了一些例外，当资本主义在农业中占主导地位时，对匪徒的理想化就结束了（2007/2000年，第198-200页）。

与海盗作斗争

海上的抢劫比陆地上更为普遍——不仅是因为欧洲政府经常参与海盗活动。这种情况在对北非海盗宣战时发生了变化。在地中海，北非的水手们得到他们统治者的祝福和利益，猎取商船。他们的目标不是货物而是船员，后者被囚禁并奴役。他们被赎回后释放（Bono 2004/2009年，第249页）。即使他们的袭击旨在囚禁水手而不是偷窃货物，这些海盗也对货物的海上运输造成了损害。

如果欧洲外交官准备为被囚禁的水手支付赎金，他们就有义务与海盗活动符合其利益的统治者谈判。他们必须同意支付。多年来，北非统治者和个别欧洲领事之间签订了和平条约和不侵犯协定？这相当于对北非统治者（deys）作为准国家结构的领主的事实承认，从17世纪起在欧洲被称为“巴巴里”国家。官方上，他们的统治者代表奥斯曼帝国行事，但实际上他们大多数时间都是独立行动的。

The Capital of Industrial Capitalism
Overseas slave economies were no precondition for the development of industrial capitalism. But the existence of slave economies influenced production and con-sumption in the capitalist mother countries. Therefore, the violence prac- 
tised against slaves has to be considered an integral part of the economy in the mother countries of capitalism.
Profits earned overseas were not a prerequisite for the development of industrial capitalism in Europe. Like others, Karl Marx was wrong when he wrote that the treasure conquered directly through plundering, enslavement and robbery and murder flowed into the mother country where it was transformed into capital (MEW, vol. 23, p. 781). Eric Williams presented a later, long-defended version of this view.15 In his 1944 work Capitalism and Slavery he argued that industrialisation in England had to a great extent been financed with profits made possible through the slave trade and the slave economy in the British colonies.'15 According to Williams, there was an interest in England in continuing the slavery of the West Indies as long as the economy depended upon profits made by importing sugar from the West Indies. Once the system was set up, however, the further development of industrialisation was no longer dependent upon these imports. Thus, interested circles in England abolished the privileges of the sugar producers. Afterwards abolitionists called for an end to the slave trade and ultimately to slavery were able to prevail (1944/1994, p. 169).17
In the 1960s, Eric Hobsbawm and Christopher Hill pursued similar arguments in their work on the development of capitalism. However, in the course of the following two decades, the position came to prevail that profits from the slave trade and the colonial economy did not play a decisive role for developing the economy in the first capitalist societies. Increasingly, the preconditions for 
the industrial revolution were seen to have been created in the mother countries themselves, and especially in England.18
Bairoch had already described the agricultural revolution as the most important precondition for capitalism. But while, for him, this role came fom the fact that investments in industrial production had been made primarily by landowners, Robert Brenner no longer focused on the issue of the capital required for industrial production but on the proletarianisation of labour linked with the agricultural revolution (1977, pp. 25-93). They thus departed from calculating profits achieved in world trade and the slave economy.
This change of interest was furthered by the observation made by Kenneth Pommeranz in particular that the investments that made the transition to industrial production possible were not initially unusually high. And also that the steam engine was thus able to take on such an enormous importance for English industry because coal mining had already been stepped up in England (2000, p. 207).
It is now the predominant view that the causes of the development of industrial capitalism are to be sought in Europe itself.19 Barring some exceptions,20 research is no longer focused on the amount of capital accumulated through the slave trade and in plantations, but on specific developments which were stimulated and promoted through the influence of international trade. The most obvious stimuli were in ship building and the development of port facilities. But without the slave trade, there would also not have been the same demand for the manufacture of so many neck irons in Liverpool. The use of copper for the ships’ hulls intended to prevent worms from attaching themselves to the ship hole2i became usual in the slave trade so as to extend the life of the ships and reduce the costs of the trade. And then the prohibition of the slave trade in the nineteenth century also furthered revolutionary changes in ship building. Because not just the transport of tea between the East Indies and England but also the transport of slaves between Africa and the West Indies 
contributed to the building of particularly fast sailing ships. If the speed of these new ships, which were the admiration of many, was useful for competition in trade with the East Indies, they were used in trade with the West Indies because slave traders had every interest in being able to flee controlling battleships (Behanzin 2002, p. 113).
In recent decades, research has been increasingly focused on connections between production and demand for individual products and the slave trade. This can be made particularly clear by reference to the production of textiles for the slave trade and the consumption of sugar produced by slaves in the West Indies.
During the heyday of the slave trade in the second half of the eighteenth century, the production of colourful ‘cotton checks’ grew in England, which were primarily used as a means of payment for buying slaves in Africa. This cloth was an imitation of checkered cloth from India which had long been produced for the West African market. From 1760 to 1780 the quantity of cotton exported to West Africa fluctuated, but what hardly changed was the share of checkered cloth of these exports. It continued to amount to 90 percent and more. It only began to sink in the following years (cf. statistics in Inikori 1992/2007, pp. 173 k). According to Joseph E. Inikori (1992/2007, pp. 156 k), the beginning of English textile production as an export branch started with these ‘checks’ and thus they were directly influenced by the slave trade. And according to Inikori it was the producers of checks who were the first to use the inventions that were to revolutionise English textile production (1992/2007, pp. 163k). It was the growth in this export sector which allowed the English textile production to surmount its previous stagnation and also contribute to the growth on the domestic market through its impact on employment (Inikori 1992/2007, p. 169).22
Ralph A. Austen and Woodruff D. Smith (1996/2007) also took leave of the earlier predominant focus on profit margins. Instead, they concentrated on the changes in patterns of consumption as an important prerequisite for developing domestic markets in industrial societies. However, in their view, the introduction of large amounts of sugar and drinks such as cocoa, coffee and tea, which were closely related to it, changed not only patterns of consumption but also the organisation and meaning of consumption. These changes can be seen in the amounts and distribution of sugar consumed, in the economic and social institutions related to this consumption and the appraisal by society of these altogether unexpected experiences (Austen and Smith 1996/2007, 
pp. 185F.). They first showed that in the eighteenth century the pro capita con-sumption of sugar rose substantially in England, even more so than in France.23 This development was related not least to the fact that in contrast to the places in which tea originated, in England, it became increasingly frequent to drink tea with sugar.24 While the consumption of coffee was promoted at the end of the seventeenth century with the institution of cafes, tea imported from India came to be used as a beverage for domestic rituals. When the price of tea began to drop at the end of the eighteenth century, persons with lower incomes would also have been able to afford tea. Its consumption - together with sugar - began to become a symbol of respectability (Austen and Smith 1996/2007, p. 193). Even if one does not follow Williams’ argument that the accumulation of capital rendered possible by the slave economy in the West Indies fuelled industrialisation, it has to be realised that the production of and trade with sugar was important for this development. For importing sugar led to the founding of consumer society and thus the longer-term development of industrial production (Austen and Smith 1996/2007, p. 197). David Richardson has pointed out that reports on changes in consumption patterns particularly refer to the capital city and that one should take general statements about the development of consumer society in England with a pinch of salt. But at the same time, he emphasises the enormous economic importance of the growing demand in sugar (Richardson 1996, p. 305). Although the prices for slaves had risen, the demand of plantation owners in the West Indies for slaves continued to grow since their revenues from tobacco, rice, indigo and especially sugar were rising (Richardson 1996, p. 308). 在十八世纪下半叶，地中海地区的袭击数量大大减少，但在法国大革命和拿破仑统治期间，这一现象再次燃起（Bono 2004/2009年，第99页）。迈克尔·肯普将对奴隶的再次大规模搜捕归因于随着欧洲和北非之间日益竞争激烈的商业关系，君主政权的经济劣势很快变得明显（2010年，第281页）。因此，代统治者，特别是阿尔及尔的代或总督委托进行了更多的袭击。与此同时，来自希腊的海盗袭击也在进行。

自美国成立以来，这样的袭击也针对寻求从地中海商人那里购买商品的美国商船进行。虽然美国政府适应了安排互惠保护信函，包括支付贡品的做法，但它也利用巴巴里海盗国的袭击为由，强迫建立自己的海军力量。最初完全是初级阶段的美国海军被授权终结对本国船只的袭击。他们1801年对巴巴里海盗国的第一次干预以美国的失败告终。然而，1805年，美军部队抵达的的黎波里，并在1819年仅凭美国海军舰只在阿尔及尔海岸附近的存在就足以获得被奴役美国水手的释放。

当时已不再谈论国家间的谈判，因为自1814至1815年在维也纳会议上呼吁结束白人奴役以来，北非统治者已成为公开的政策目标。现在，英国皇家海军也被派往地中海，理由是它在打击奴隶贸易。当法国在1830年征服阿尔及利亚时，巴巴里海盗被提出为一个重要原因。实际上，到了大约1815年，海盗活动已经被放弃。

在世界上最重要的货物运输路线上，国家海军的介入限制了通过海盗进行的占有。在十九世纪，这种国家垄断通过发展能够不受风向影响追捕和猎取海盗的蒸汽船而得到加强。就像陆地上的强盗团伙一样，这种对海盗活动的遏制导致了传奇和理想化。

工业资本主义的资本

海外奴隶经济不是工业资本主义发展的先决条件。但奴隶经济的存在影响了资本主义母国的生产和消费。因此，对奴隶施加的暴力必须被视为资本主义母国经济不可分割的一部分。

海外赚取的利润不是欧洲发展工业资本主义的先决条件。像其他人一样，卡尔·马克思在他写道通过掠夺、奴役、抢劫和谋杀直接征服的财富流入母国，并转化为资本时是错误的（MEW，第23卷，第781页）。埃里克·威廉斯后来提出了这一观点的长期捍卫版本。在他1944年的作品《资本主义与奴隶制》中，他认为英国的工业化在很大程度上是用通过奴隶贸易和英国殖民地的奴隶经济可能获得的利润资助的。据威廉斯称，只要经济依赖于从西印度群岛进口糖的利润，英国就有兴趣继续西印度群岛的奴隶制。然而，一旦体系建立，工业化的进一步发展就不再依赖这些进口。因此，英国有兴趣人士废除了糖生产商的特权。之后，废奴主义者呼吁结束奴隶贸易，最终呼吁废除奴隶制得以占上风（1944/1994年，第169页）。

20世纪60年代，埃里克·霍布斯鲍姆和克里斯托弗·希尔在他们关于资本主义发展的作品中提出了类似的论点。然而，在接下来的二十年里，开始占据主导地位的观点是，奴隶贸易和殖民经济的利润对发展第一个资本主义社会的经济没有起决定性作用。越来越多地认为，工业革命的先决条件是在母国，特别是在英国本身创造的。

贝罗什已经将农业革命描述为资本主义最重要的先决条件。但对他来说，这个作用来自于工业生产的投资主要由地主进行，而罗伯特·布伦纳不再关注工业生产所需资本的问题，而是关注与农业革命相关的劳动力无产阶级化（1977年，第25-93页）。他们因此偏离了计算在世界贸易和奴隶经济中获得的利润。

由于特别是肯尼斯·庞梅朗兹的观察，这种兴趣转变得到了推进，即过渡到工业生产所需的投资最初并不通常高。同时，由于在英国已经加强了煤矿开采，蒸汽机因此能够对英国工业产生如此巨大的影响（2000年，第207页）。

现在普遍认为，工业资本主义的发展原因应在欧洲本身寻找。尽管存在一些例外，研究不再聚焦于通过奴隶贸易和种植园积累的资本量，而是聚焦于通过国际贸易的影响激发和促进的特定发展。最明显的刺激在于造船和港口设施的发展。但如果没有奴隶贸易，也不会有那么多在利物浦制造的颈铁的需求。用于船体的铜板，旨在防止虫子附着在船体上，起初在奴隶贸易中变得常见，以延长船只的使用寿命，降低贸易成本。然后，19世纪对奴隶贸易的禁止也促进了造船业的革命性变化。因为不仅印度和英国之间的茶叶运输，还有非洲和西印度群岛之间的奴隶运输，促成了特别快速的帆船建造。如果这些新船的速度使得它们在与印度的贸易竞争中非常有用，那么在与西印度群岛的贸易中使用它们是因为奴隶贸易商非常希望能够逃避巡逻的战舰（Behanzin 2002年，第113页）。

近几十年来，研究越来越多地聚焦于生产和对个别产品需求与奴隶贸易之间的联系。这可以通过提到为奴隶贸易生产的纺织品和通过奴隶在西印度群岛生产的糖的消费来特别清楚地说明。

在十八世纪下半叶奴隶贸易的鼎盛时期，英国生产的彩色“棉布格子”数量增加，这些主要用作在非洲购买奴隶的支付手段。这种布是对长期以来为西非市场生产的印度格子布的模仿。从1760年到1780年，出口到西非的棉花数量波动，但几乎没有改变的是这些出口中格子布的份额。它持续占到90%以上。仅在随后的几年里开始下降（参见Inikori 1992/2007年，第173k页的统计数据）。据Joseph E. Inikori（1992/2007年，第156k页）称，英国纺织品生产作为出口部门的开始是与这些“格子”直接相关的，因此直接受到奴隶贸易的影响。而且据Inikori称，是生产格子的制造商首先使用了将革命化英国纺织品生产的发明（1992/2007年，第163k页）。这一出口部门的增长使英国纺织品生产克服了之前的停滞，并通过其对就业的影响，也为国内市场的增长做出了贡献（Inikori 1992/2007年，第169页）。

Ralph A. Austen和Woodruff D. Smith（1996/2007年）也摆脱了早期普遍关注利润率的做法。相反，他们专注于消费模式的变化作为发展工业社会国内市场的重要先决条件。然而，在他们看来，大量糖和与之密切相关的如可可、咖啡和茶等饮品的引入，不仅改变了消费模式，也改变了消费的组织和意义。这些变化可以从糖的消费量和分布、与这种消费相关的经济和社会机构以及社会对这些完全出乎意料的经验的评价中看出（Austen和Smith 1996/2007年，第185F页）。他们首先表明，在十八世纪，英国的人均糖消费量显著增加，甚至超过了法国。这一发展与英国与茶原产地相比，越来越频繁地喝加糖的茶有关。而咖啡的消费在17世纪末随着咖啡馆的设立而得到推广，从印度进口的茶成为家庭仪式的饮料。当18世纪末茶叶价格开始下降时，低收入人群也可以负担得起茶叶。其消费——连同糖——开始成为体面的象征（Austen和Smith 1996/2007年，第193页）。即使不接受威廉斯的论点，即西印度群岛的奴隶经济通过积累的资本推动了工业化，也必须认识到糖的生产和贸易对这一发展很重要。因为进口糖导致了消费社会的建立，从而对工业生产的长期发展起到了推动作用（Austen和Smith 1996/2007年，第197页）。David Richardson指出，关于消费模式变化的报道特别提到了首都，人们对英国消费社会发展的一般性陈述应持保留态度。但与此同时，他强调了糖需求增长的巨大经济重要性（Richardson 1996年，第305页）。尽管奴隶价格上涨，西印度群岛种植园主对奴隶的需求继续增长，因为他们从烟草、大米、靛蓝，尤其是糖的收入在增加（Richardson 1996年，第308页）。虽然英国奴隶贸易商也向北美供应奴隶，但大多数奴隶被供应给西印度群岛。随着糖出口收入的增加，不仅种植园主更愿意用新奴隶替换死去的奴隶，甚至购买额外的奴隶，而且对英国产品的需求也在西印度群岛种植园主中增加。自18世纪初以来，英国出口的制成品份额显著增长。Richardson得出结论，英国的工业化不仅受到国内市场需求的推动，还受到从奴隶劳动中获利的西印度群岛建园主的大量需求的推动（1996年，第320-2页）。由于从十八世纪中叶开始，英国对非洲、东印度和新世界的出口95%由制成品组成（Richardson 1996年，第331页），可以假设英国糖消费的增长在工业化发展中发挥了重要作用。

From Merchant Capital to the Trading Capital of Industrial Capitalism
The most important precondition for the structural transformation of trade was its disembedding from the structures of rule of the Ancien Régime.
In the 1730s, Charles 1 of England lost the support of major merchants and factory owners through his policy of privileges. That he was not able to find any creditors for the violent confrontations with Parliament beginning in 1640 exacerbated the monarchical crisis that was to lead to the revolution of 1649.27 The revolutionary abolition of the monarchy led to the abolition of the prerogative to award monopolies (patents).
However, together with the restoration of the monarchy, the prerogative to grant privileges was also restored. The most important of these privileges was the British East India Company’s trading monopoly over India. The company managed not only to defend the monopoly throughout the political revolution, but even to be awarded civil and military jurisdiction over India. It thus became a sort of private colonial state. The India Bill of 1784 formalised this status. In 1818, the company’s monopoly over trade with India came to an end. Thus, the freedom of trade in British possessions introduced for British subjects as early as 1760 was now extended to India. 
Even before then, a de facto competition among British merchants had already emerged in trade with India. In formal terms, such competition was possible wherever commercial freedom was introduced. But this did not suffice to transform merchant capital into capitalist trading capital. This structural transformation only took place when it was no longer inevitable that the sales of products and the procurement of raw materials was carried out by specialised companies. For the privileges of merchants were not just derived from on high, but also through their privileged position of disposing of the methods and relations for concluding trading transactions. Producers who did not want to restrict their trade to the narrowest geographic environment thus depended upon trading companies. For this reason - as Marx pointed out almost in an aside in a brief chapter - the profit of merchants tended to be higher than that of craftsmen (MEW 25, pp. 335-49). Since industrial companies have become potentially able to organise their supplies and sales themselves, the prices of trading companies compete with the costs that industrial companies calculate they would incur if they organised these activities themselves. It is this competition that transformed trading capital into one form of capital among others.
The Liberation of Wage Labour from Coercive Political Power
In capitalist production, labour is used under conditions of competition. This became more possible on a larger scale in the first European countries to become capitalist when many people were forced to procure provisions for themselves and their families through wage labour. If they were legally free, they were able to offer their labour power to employers (in German ‘givers of work’, Arbeitgeber). However, contrary to widespread assumption, this did not immediately mean that labour was detached from control by authorities. That liberation had to be won through political struggle. It is not one of the founding characteristics ofcapital- ism.
For centuries, labour in Europe had been subject to personal legal power. The forms varied from one principality, monastery or town to the next, and also varied over the course of time. However, one generalisation is possible: whoever was not a merchant or a master craftsman, an itinerant scholar, a cleric or an academic, was very probably either a master or a mistress, a manservant or a maidservant, a journeyman or an apprentice. Even the servants at a princely court, even the most famous musicians, subjugated themselves to a relationship of domination. In other words, they not only had to perform the 
obligations to which they had committed themselves, but they also had to comply with far-reaching regulations of their behaviour.
In their relations to labourers, princes, farmers, master craftsmen or burghers were lords in their houses. They thus could and should lord over everyone working for them just as they would over their wives, their daughters and their minor sons. Rule over a household was personal rule and at the same time it was a controlling rule that should contribute to maintaining the social order (Steindl 1984, p. 85). It included the right to corporal punishment.
If at the turn from the eighteenth to the nineteenth century ‘agrarian workers in Prussia were obliged to accept and in fact did accept being beaten, thrashed and drubbed without resistance’, this was because they could not escape this brutality. The right for lords to engage in ‘moderate’ corporal punishment was again authorised by the state in the General Law of the State (Allgemeines Landrecht) introduced in 1794.28
In France, not all but very many landowners exercised lower jurisdiction over the unfree peasants who were their subjects, and some of them even had the right to decide over life and death. In England, peasants had been legally free for centuries. Most of them worked as day labourers or tenants on the land of lords. Individual landowners did not hold legal jurisdiction. Instead, the practice of lower legal jurisdiction lay in the hands of the justices of the peace. Justices of the peace were appointed by the Crown and were well-to-do and locally prestigious landowners. They had not studied law, but practised law as seen to be appropriate by themselves and their ‘neighbours’ (Gerstenberger 1990/2007, pp. 254-7). However, cases occurred in which a justice of the peace would have one of his workers whose behaviour he disapproved of imprisoned or flogged.29

To maintain order, masters could make use of their own coercive power over their household and also of the coercieve power of the state. agriculture.30 We shall therefore pursue this assumption and shall begin our discussion of historical examples by considering the development of dependent labour in the economically comparatively backward country of Prussia.
